Free browser tools that never see your data
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Last week I needed to share a Mermaid diagram with colleagues — on Teams, in a Confluence page, the usual places where “just paste the code” doesn’t cut it. I needed an image. Simple task.
I didn’t even look at the online converters. Uploading an architecture diagram — internal service names, data flows, the works — to some website I’d never heard of? Hard pass. So I wrote a Python script. It worked. It was also annoying enough that I kept thinking: there has to be a way to do this without choosing between convenience and not shipping your data off to who-knows-where.
About ten hours later, jugad.tools was live — starting with the Mermaid tool I wished I’d had that morning.
Tools that work for you, not on you
The idea is stupidly simple: useful dev utilities that run entirely in your browser. No ads. No accounts. No “we’ll email you the result.” Your data never leaves your device — not your JWT, not your Parquet file, not that JSON config you’re embarrassed to admit you hand-edited.
I called it jugad — a Hindi word for clever improvisation when the proper solution doesn’t exist or takes too long. Which felt… accurate.
Where it started
The Mermaid tool was first — diagram → PNG → paste into Teams or Confluence. That was the itch. Once it worked, I kept going with the same rule: useful, local, no upload.
Two I reach for constantly
JWT — When auth breaks at 2am, you need to decode a token. The payload has user IDs, roles, internal claims — stuff that really shouldn’t sit on someone else’s server. I’m not pasting that into a random decoder site, even if “everyone does it.” Decode and verify locally, with your own secret or public key, and move on.
Markdown — I draft READMEs and internal runbooks with GFM tables and task lists. Preview locally before they land in Confluence or a repo. No uploading a half-baked doc to someone’s preview server just to see if the formatting looks right.
What’s in the box
I grouped everything so you can find what you need without scrolling through a junk drawer:
Data — Format and validate JSON. Clean up JSONL for fine-tuning runs. Diff text, JSON, or YAML side by side. Peek at Parquet schemas without uploading your dataset. Run DuckDB SQL on CSV, JSON, JSONL, or Parquet files locally. (Yes, real SQL in the browser. No, I didn’t think I’d need this either until I did.)
AI & ML — Count GPT-style tokens before you blow your context window. Compare text similarity and rank RAG chunks with local embeddings. Pull answer spans from a document with extractive Q&A. All of it runs in-browser. No API keys. No “send us your prompt.”
Security — Decode and verify JWTs. Generate passwords and diceware passphrases. Mint UUIDs v1 through v7. The stuff you need weekly and hate pasting into random sites.
Create — Merge PDFs. Live Markdown preview with GFM tables. Mermaid diagrams. QR codes for links, WiFi, whatever.
Why bother?
Because the “free online tool” model usually means your data becomes their problem to mishandle. Because you shouldn’t have to choose between convenience and not shipping internals — architecture diagrams, JWT payloads, draft docs — to a site you’ll never audit.
jugad.tools is free. It stays in your browser. I built it for developer friends who shouldn’t have to think twice before pasting something sensitive.
Take a look. Tell me what’s missing. I’m still improvising.

Medium tags: developer-tools, privacy, build-in-public, web-development, open-source
